parts:
Here's the best parts of a recent podcast. What I want you to do is do a deep dive to find the following so we can improve on what was mentioned:
1. Research the speaker (Bill Gurley) so we can compile some more context.
2. About each segment, look into the facts or the patterns mentioned - not just to factcheck, but also to add more color and interesting things. That might be similar quotes, contemporaries at the time, competing dynamics, new information about the time, ho right they are and what the implications are, how they can be connected to today's situation, etc.
3. Don't look into the obvious things. The intended audience is people in the field, so not too many of the generic well known things (though you can use them in a sentence or two to add context), but things that make you go 'ah I didn't know that'.
Then compile a research report with each segment and additional elaboration. Feel free to be as verbose as you can to add context.

Here is a transcript of the most useful and important parts of the podcast.

### **0:00 Intro**

**Host:** So Bill, this marks you retaking the crown as the most frequent Invest Like the Best guest, beating our good friend Michael Mauboussin out for the crown. Welcome back.

**Bill Gurley:** I can't think of anyone I'd rather be neck-and-neck with on the entire planet.

**Host:** And this is also interesting that this is the first one that you and I have done, just the two of us, since 2019, if you can believe that.

### **0:23 State of the Union: VC Edition**

**Host:** Um, since it's just us, I I would love to go very broad and start the conversation by just talking about the state of things as you see them. I know this is something you used to do in in your Benchmark days, was sort of give like a State of the Union Markets edition. I would love for you to do that for us as we enter summer of 2025 with everything that you're seeing out there in the world.

**Bill Gurley:** I'm excited to do it. Yeah, I used to kick off our LP meeting with a state of VC. Um, so it's a, it's a, it's a process and a presentation I'm used to giving. And I've been noticing a lot of things recently that are different in the world and maybe permanently different about the venture capital world. And a lot of the talks that I would give, um, were based on what appeared to be inherent cyclicality in in the venture business, and that has been kind of upset or kicked over or a little chaotic recently, which we'll get into.

### **2:58 The Rise of Mega VC Funds**

**Bill Gurley:** So, let me walk through the realities and then you and I can chat back and forth about some of the interpretations of those. So, the first thing I would just bring up, which people have talked about, so I'm I'm I'm just putting it on the table as one of the key variables, not trying to overanalyze it, which is the continued rise of the mega VC fund. When I first started, you know, everything was bespoke. Most, most of the well-branded funds were focused on early stage. Um, they didn't participate in late stage. Um, and the funds were modest compared to today. Um, today, many of the branded firms, I think, have moved from maybe 500 million commitment every three or four years to 5 billion. You know, so, so 10X. And they're they're participating in very actively in what we would call late stage. Although I I've always thought late stage was a euphemism for big check, you know.

### **4:38 Zombie Unicorns**

**Bill Gurley:** I think the phrase that's used most, which I don't love, is "zombie unicorn." So, if you look at the number of companies that were, I always like to think pre-LLM and post-LLM, because it really is kind of a dividing moment as everyone's gotten excited about this new platform shift. Um, there's somewhere around a thousand. So these are a thousand private companies that have raised money over a billion dollars. And some have it, ChatGPT told me it was 1250, NVCA says 900. Let's just say it's near a thousand. I think the number of total public companies in the US is way down from peak. And so there's, there's even, there's less companies going public.

### **12:29 The IPO and M&A Market Stalemate**

**Bill Gurley:** The next one's IPO, you know, like exits. Like, where are these things are going to actually, you know, price in a real way.

**Host:** Yeah.

**Bill Gurley:** So we got mega fund, zombie unicorns, and then the capital markets. So for reasons that aren't, I think, well-articulated, well-understood, both the IPO and M&A markets have stalled, um, over the past couple of years. 2021 was actually pretty good, um, on both fronts. Um, but things have stalled. I think it's really important, if you look at last year, 2024, the Nasdaq was up 30%, and the window was closed. Or that seems to be the general belief of everyone out there. Never in my history of paying attention to the capital markets or being in venture capital do you have a successful Nasdaq market and a closed window. No IPOs, yeah. Yeah, it makes no sense. Those, that was what was correlated. And so something else is happening, you know. Um, I I I believe, you know, a part of it, which, you know, I've been very passionate about, is the IPO discount that the banks, you know, force upon the market, especially the well-known high-branded ones.

### **19:08 The AI Wave and Its Impact**

**Bill Gurley:** The AI wave came at a really interesting time. This is this is my my fifth point, I think, out of six realities. Um, we were headed to this mini correction, and if you, you have to remember, Patrick, people were tightening their belt. These companies were laying people off, they were trying to get to break even. There was worried about them being able to raise money. And I would argue over the past, you know, 30 years that I have a window into VC, the corrections were healthy. Like every time the VC community got out over its skis, there'd be a correction and things would settle back down. Um, you never had a full correction here because AI came along and everyone got so excited. And I'm not saying they shouldn't be excited. Like, if it is the biggest platform shift in our lifetime, then you have to get excited about it. It it has implications for the zombie unicorn group and everything else if if if that's true. But we've all of a sudden seen massive amount of interest in investing. We're seeing companies, you know, the AI companies, what do you think? They're what, their valuations relative to revenue, what? 10x, 20x a normal company? Is that fair?

### **21:03 Private Markets and LP Liquidity Issues**

**Bill Gurley:** The next big thing we're talking about is many LPs, not all LPs, but many LPs have a liquidity problem. And that's a new reality. It's tied to the lack of IPOs and the lack of M&A. Um, it's also, you know, it's also fairly new and unique. So in the first quarter of 2025, US colleges and universities issued $12 billion of debt, which was the third highest quarter ever. And so that that's an interesting reality if you're using debt to fund capital commitments because your endowment doesn't have the liquidity it needs to pay out the 3% or 5% or whatever it is that they always had traditionally paid out. And then very recently, you probably saw this, Harvard, you know, announced they're in the market selling secondary for a billion. They have a lot of unique things that would make them be out there. But even more interesting, Yale has announced that they're in the market looking to sell $6 billion of private equity. The fact that Yale is the one doing it is super important and super interesting from a historical perspective.

### **24:57 The Future of Capital Markets**

**Host:** I want to talk about LPs now and the tendency or lack thereof for capital to seek the highest risk-adjusted return in general. It should be the case, like in a rational sense, that over time the pools of capital would shift around and seek the highest risk-adjusted return. Like, that's the whole point. And so I'm curious what you think the impediments are to that just happening, and it's another way of asking like, what should LPs do now? Like, they're the capital owners, or they represent the capital owners. Their job ostensibly is to get the best risk-adjusted returns relative to their their own personal needs. What should they do and what might stop them from doing it?

**Bill Gurley:** This will probably be the last point that I'd love to to drive home, and then we can just talk broadly about the situation. But you ask a provocative question at the very beginning of the podcast, or early on, you said, you know, could the LP liquidity issue be a catalyst of some kind that that causes this this world to change? And there are a lot of things pressing at that. Um, time is a problem, as we've already talked about, and they've been putting debt in place. Um, there's broad talk in Washington about endowment taxes, which drives more liquidity requirements for these endowments, and it's something they've never had before. Um, you have the research cuts, not just the aggressive stuff at Harvard, but even the the research cuts on the um on the normal NIH and NSF grants.

### **32:49 Advice for Founders in a Changing Landscape**

**Host:** I I I'd love to play with with one piece of this that I think is really important before I get back to asking the game on the field question for GPs and LPs, which is really just to get your take on AI as a new general-purpose enabling technology. Because that is the key difference between now and 2021. It it it certainly does. I mean, we've never seen, just like we've never seen rounds like this, we've also never seen revenue ramps like this in companies.

**Bill Gurley:** True.

**Host:** And so so it does appear to have lots of hallmarks of, I mean, I'm sure, I know you're like me because you love technology. I use this stuff all day every day. It's the most amazing technology I've ever encountered.

**Bill Gurley:** So do I. So do I.

### **34:27 The High-Stakes Game of Capital Battles**

**Bill Gurley:** What ends up happening in this world, because it's the same thing that happened in 2021, is the minute there's a company that has any amount of excitement about it whatsoever, someone's knocking on the door trying to give them 100, 200, $300 million. And what that does is it forces everyone to go all or nothing, swing for the fences. And I lived it in the Uber-Lyft situation, but we're going to have that type of capital battle in every category under the sun. And you you know, you mentioned the notion of like traditional company building. Traditional company building isn't spend 100 or $150 million a year in cash burn. But all the big AI companies are doing that, maybe more. I think OpenAI said they're going to spend $7 billion in a year. Um, and that's not, you know, your grandfather's startup business or your grandfather's venture capital. That's a radically different world. And if you're a founder, you'd like to think the advice is, well, ignore all that and build your company the way you want to build it. But if your competitor raises $300 million and is going to 10x the size of their sales force or 50x it, you will be dead before you know it. Like, you won't be around. So you are forced to play the game on the field.

### **37:57 Challenges & Opportunities in AI Revenue Models**

**Bill Gurley:** There's another negative element of all the systems issues that we're talking about is the the firms that that begged their way onto the cap chart by writing a $300 million check or whatever, they differentiate themselves by being the best friend of the founder they possibly can be. They don't take a responsibility of being someone to help you make better decisions. They're never going to tell you no.

### **39:37 The Role of Founders in the AI Revolution**

**Host:** Maybe kind of in closing, you could leave leave people with a few thoughts on for founders specifically. I always like to try to come back to them because without them doing their thing, like this doesn't matter at all. None of it matters. Founders are facing maybe the best setup of all time with more tools to build companies, the most exciting new enabling technology maybe we've ever seen, lots of capital that's willing to fund that journey. Um, any closing thoughts? You know, you've seen so many founders build some amazing, you know, big businesses across your career, backed a lot of them directly. Closing thoughts for them on the opportunity in front of them and how they should think about it?

**Bill Gurley:** Yeah, just, you know, if you're if you're lucky enough to be in one of these hot companies, you know, and and you're in the middle of this whole world that we talked about, um, I would offer a couple things. One, unit economics will matter one day. And and and that doesn't mean you have to sharpen the pencil right now. Like I said, the two generation old models cost 1/100th of the token. You can plan that we're going to move to that and you can it's it's fine. Like, I think that's fine to have a burn rate, but the unit economics will eventually matter.

### **41:44 The Impact of Time and Liquidity on Venture Capital**

**Bill Gurley:** The other thing I'd love to drive home is that time is a massive problem. And so, you know, we're moving the time to liquidity of these companies from uh 5 to 7 years to 10 to 15 years. I don't know the exact number, and I and I think, you know, every LP is aware of that. You have the cost of capital, the IRR that just eats away. And everyone loves to say, oh, it's not IRR, it's DPI. But if time doubles, it it is IRR. Like, if that's what really matters. And um and in addition to that time, uh the cost of capital, you have dilution. So every one of these zombie unicorns is diluting 3, 4, 5, 6% a year on equity issuance uh to the employee base. And when you combine those two, it's a real, you know, it's a real problem. So if you take um, let's say you you were expecting to get $100 back from an investment in year 10, and you wanted to delay it to year 15, if you just take that 10% compounding, you it now needs to be worth $160, you know, dollars out in year 15.

### **45:35 Navigating the Future of Venture Capital**

**Bill Gurley:** The big companies seem to have moved very quickly. I mean, if you go on, you know, ServiceNow's website, it just drips of AI. You know, Microsoft earnings transcript had 67 occurrences of AI. You know, and Satya just talked for two hours about AI. Um, it's a it's a weird thing. A lot, I think a lot of what we read, you know, in in Crossing the Chasm or or The Innovator's Dilemma, you know, the big companies are supposed to be slow to mobile, slow to the internet, slow, and that's an opportunity for the startups. This is an interesting one where I think a lot of the big companies have paid attention early.

### **53:45 International Dynamics in the AI Race**

**Bill Gurley:** When DeepSeek hit and took off, we were all focused on how the US reacted, the US models, Washington, you know, the fact that AWS, you know, hosted DeepSeek or whatever. What happened in China, however, is Alibaba made Quinn um open source. Uh, Xiaomi has a model out now, um, I forget the name of it, Moby, I think maybe. It's open source. And um Robin Li, Baidu, had kept his model proprietary and he said in June it's going to be open source. And so that level of competition, if it leads to four deep-pocketed all open products, um, is going to be super powerful. And we've already learned um that these models can train each other and help each other get better. So if you have four open ones that can all train on each other and everybody can get a hold of that, I think that's going to lead to a massive amount of optionality and experimentation that we're not going to have here. Um, so I'm that's the most fascinating piece of the international AI um narrative that I've seen.

### **1:08:58 Advice for Founders in the AI Era**

**Bill Gurley:** As you grow and get over 100 million and get to a billion in revenue, you just can't operate, and this is advice in any cycle really, but it gets accentuated here because of the amount of capital. I I thought, you know, one of my favorite pieces that Reid Hoffman ever wrote, he wrote about Uber, this pirate navy metaphor, where he said all all startups are born as pirates, but they eventually have to become a navy. And that's true, and I think it's I think it's an uncomfortable transition for some. Um, but you have to figure out a way to do it. You have to want to do that. And I you know, that's there's a personality type where that's very hard. You know, and I've I've had great conversation with Michael Dell, he did it. For a while, he thought he didn't want to do it, ended up finding a way where he could do it and be happy, and it's hard. That part's hard. Um, and then maybe the last two things. One, network effects are real and they can be more real if you focus on them.

# Bill Gurley and the 2025 Venture Capital Landscape

## Background: Bill Gurley’s Career and Influence

Bill Gurley is one of Silicon Valley’s most renowned venture capitalists, known for his long tenure at Benchmark Capital and a keen analytical eye on industry trends. A former Wall Street tech analyst and engineer, Gurley joined Benchmark in 1999 and became its longest-serving partner【3†L4-L7】. Over two decades he led early investments in numerous successful startups – **Uber** (Benchmark’s $11 million check in 2011 became one of VC’s legendary bets【3†L4-L7】), **GrubHub**, **Zillow**, **OpenTable**, **Stitch Fix**, **Nextdoor**, among others【4†L178-L186】【4†L188-L191】. These ventures cemented his reputation, landing him consistently on the Forbes *Midas List* of top tech investors【4†L188-L191】. Gurley was not just an early backer of iconic companies but also a vocal thought leader via his blog *Above the Crowd*. He often sounded alarms on unsustainable market behaviors – for instance, warning in 2015 that *“venture-backed businesses have moved into a world that is both speculative and unsustainable”*【4†L199-L204】. Colleagues respect him for combining big-picture “systems level” thinking with hard-won pattern recognition from past boom-bust cycles. Notably, while at Benchmark he would annually present a “State of the Union” to their investors, dissecting market realities – a tradition he continued in a **June 2025** appearance on *Invest Like the Best*. In that wide-ranging discussion (his sixth on the podcast), Gurley delivered a comprehensive analysis of the forces reshaping venture capital【6†L45-L53】【6†L73-L81】. The sections below expand on the key themes he addressed – from the rise of giant VC funds to “zombie” unicorn startups and beyond – incorporating data and perspectives from across the industry to enrich Gurley’s insights.

## The Rise of Mega VC Funds

One striking change Gurley highlights is the **explosive growth in venture fund size and capital supply**. In his early career, VC firms were relatively small and specialized – mostly early-stage focused with fund sizes in the low hundreds of millions. Today, top venture franchises routinely raise **“mega-funds” in the billions** of dollars【7†L156-L164】. Gurley notes many well-known firms have expanded their fund commitments tenfold (from ~$500M a decade ago to $5B+ now) and now aggressively participate in late-stage deals【7†L160-L168】. In fact, the average VC fund size nearly doubled over the past decade – from ~$84M in 2013 to $154M by late 2023【14†L139-L147】 – as an unprecedented volume of capital flowed into the asset class. Venture capital’s total assets under management (AUM) surged from about **$300 billion in 2008 to $3.5 *trillion* in 2022**【14†L129-L137】, making VC a mainstream investment category (now roughly a quarter of all private capital AUM【14†L129-L137】). This influx was fueled by years of low interest rates (ZIRP) and strong VC returns attracting major pensions, endowments, and sovereign wealth funds seeking higher yield【14†L123-L131】【14†L130-L138】. 

**Mega-funds** such as Andreessen Horowitz’s multiple billion-dollar funds, Tiger Global’s crossover funds, and SoftBank’s $100B Vision Fund have fundamentally “recalibrated” the venture game【14†L139-L147】. With so much money to put to work, these players blurred the lines between traditional venture and growth equity – writing huge checks even into young startups. Gurley quips that “late-stage” investing often just means *large* financings: e.g. a $300 million round for a 12-month-old AI company is hardly “late stage” in any classical sense【7†L162-L170】. The result is more capital chasing deals at every phase. Over the past decade, valuations swelled dramatically across stages: the *average* pre-money valuation for a seed round tripled from ~$5M in 2013 to ~$18M in 2023【14†L175-L183】, and Series A/B valuations jumped similarly【14†L175-L183】. Round sizes followed suit, forcing VCs to deploy larger checks to maintain ownership positions【14†L169-L177】【14†L185-L193】. In short, *“big money”* fundamentally changed the venture financing environment.

This trend has *implications for returns and market dynamics*. By definition, bigger funds need bigger outcomes – a huge fund must spawn multiple $10B-$50B+ exits to deliver venture-like returns【14†L107-L115】【14†L139-L147】. That raises the stakes and may encourage **riskier behavior** (chasing “winner-take-all” markets, tolerating sky-high burn rates) in pursuit of outsized payoffs. It also creates a **“haves vs. have-nots” dynamic** among VCs themselves: large firms hoover up a greater share of LP dollars and can outbid smaller funds in later rounds, potentially squeezing traditional early-stage firms【39†L62-L69】【39†L63-L66】. At the same time, even some elite VCs question the mega-fund arms race. Gurley’s own firm Benchmark pointedly resisted swelling its fund size in boom times, continuing to raise modest-sized funds to preserve discipline and the chance for high multiples【14†L155-L163】. Nonetheless, the industry’s center of gravity shifted. 

Huge multi-stage funds and new entrants (crossover hedge funds, mutual funds, sovereign wealth funds, corporate venture arms) now compete heavily in private deals【7†L169-L177】. SoftBank’s Vision Fund, for example, injected unheard-of sums into startups like WeWork and DoorDash, driving valuations to extremes. Tiger Global and Coatue became infamous for blitzscaling investments – writing term sheets in days and often paying premium prices to win hot deals. This **flood of capital** during the 2015–2021 bull run bid up startup valuations across the board【14†L169-L177】. By 2021’s peak, VC financing was at record froth: global venture fundraising hit an all-time high of $363B that year【14†L131-L139】, and investors were funding even concept-stage companies at $100M+ valuations in some cases. The **feedback loop** was self-reinforcing – as venture funds grew larger, they *had* to deploy more capital per deal, which inflated valuations and required later investors to raise even bigger funds.

From a historical perspective, the rise of mega-funds mirrors trends seen in private equity buyouts a decade earlier, but is relatively new to venture capital. One concern is whether venture returns can keep pace with this asset class expansion. There is evidence that venture performance, once *non-correlated* with size, is becoming harder to sustain at scale – many of the best returns in VC history came from relatively small funds backing companies at modest entry prices. The 2022–2023 market correction provided an early test: some mega-funds that aggressively invested at 2021 valuations (e.g. Tiger Global) reportedly faced steep markdowns and losses when the bubble deflated. Gurley and others have argued that oversized funds may end up **absorbing index-like returns** (or worse) if they continuously overpay for growth. In the long run, LPs may question whether the largest VC funds can justify their size via outsized alpha.

Yet in 2024–25, mega-funds continue to shape the landscape. **More money is in the system than ever**【7†L175-L182】. This abundance has some upsides: it enables ambitious founders to raise capital for truly transformative ideas (e.g. AI or space startups that need huge sums), and it blurs the once-rigid boundaries between venture and public markets (late-stage “private IPO” rounds now provide liquidity without an actual IPO – more on that below). But it also means **stiffer competition** and potentially diminished capital efficiency. As Gurley observed, too much money can be a curse: it can breed complacency and strategic bloat. There’s research and anecdote to support this – plentiful funding often leads startups to attempt *“doing seven projects”* instead of focusing, whereas constraints force creativity【7†L273-L281】. In the late 2010s, many richly funded unicorns (from **Jawbone** to **Quibi**) famously burned cash on growth-at-all-costs strategies that never yielded sustainable businesses. The mega-fund era arguably amplified such cases. Gurley’s bottom line is that the venture ecosystem has permanently been altered by the sheer scale of capital – a reality every participant now must navigate.

## Zombie Unicorns: The Overvalued Giants Stuck in Limbo

The term **“zombie unicorn”** has entered the VC lexicon to describe private startups valued at $1B+ that are *alive* (in that they continue operating) but effectively *dead* to new investors and unable to achieve a successful exit. Gurley calls this phenomenon – the glut of overfunded, overvalued unicorns – one of the stark “market realities” today. By mid-2025, there are roughly **1,200+ private companies valued over $1 billion** globally【21†L43-L51】【21†L81-L89】, an astonishing figure up from only a few dozen a decade prior. Collectively these unicorns have raised on the order of **$1 *trillion*** in capital and, on paper, are valued well above $4-$5 trillion in aggregate【17†L195-L200】. Yet many of them are languishing. The IPO window for high-growth startups essentially slammed shut after late 2021, leaving a record backlog of billion-dollar companies “waiting to go public” – or waiting for some liquidation event – with no clear timeline【21†L75-L83】【21†L93-L100】. The *Inc.* magazine dryly noted in early 2025 that **1,200 such unicorns have become “dead weight”** in the private markets【21†L43-L51】【21†L81-L89】.

Why are so many richly valued startups now “walking dead”? A big factor is the **valuation excesses of 2020–2021**. During that euphoric period (fueled by zero rates and a COVID-era tech boom), investors rewarded growth over profits, allowing money-losing companies to raise successive rounds at ever-higher valuations. In 2021 alone, more than **350 new unicorns were minted**, but fewer than 2% of those managed to go public by 2025【21†L93-L100】. The vast majority are still private – and for many, their last funding round (often in 2021) was at a valuation no longer supportable by fundamentals in the colder light of 2023–2024【7†L201-L210】. Gurley points out that a huge number of unicorns **“last priced themselves in 2021”**, at the peak of the market【7†L201-L210】. Since then, public market comps (high-growth tech stocks) have fallen, and many of these startups saw growth *decelerate* (especially those that benefited from pandemic lockdown dynamics like e-commerce or remote work). Were they to raise capital today, they would likely be priced far lower – but they *haven’t* raised, precisely to avoid that fate. Thus they exist in a kind of valuation limbo, with their **“book” (last-round) valuations diverging drastically from any realistic market value**.

The **incentive structure** in venture land unfortunately encourages this charade. Gurley bluntly states that *no one* – not the general partners (VCs), not the limited partner investors, not the founders – has a real incentive to mark these companies down to their true value【7†L213-L221】【7†L223-L231】. Venture firms typically carry portfolio companies at the last round price; writing them down would hurt their track record and threaten their ability to raise the next fund. Even LPs, who ultimately want honest valuations, sometimes prefer illusion over crystalized losses – many large LPs award staff bonuses based on portfolio book values, creating a **perverse incentive to “play along”**【7†L223-L231】【7†L232-L240】. Founders, meanwhile, are psychologically anchored to those peak valuations – Gurley wryly notes that most founders equate their personal net worth with their ownership * times* the highest paper valuation achieved【7†L232-L240】. Admitting that the company is now worth 70% less is not only painful to the ego, it could wipe out employees’ morale and risk press coverage of “down rounds” or “failures.” Moreover, many late-stage financings came with hefty **liquidation preferences** – a company that raised, say, $300M at a $2B valuation might find that if it sells for only $400M now, **investors reclaim almost all proceeds** (the $300M preference stack) leaving little for the team【7†L240-L249】. This overhang discourages founders from accepting low exit offers or recapitalizations; they instead *keep limping along*, hoping something improves.

Thus the zombies shuffle on. They are **companies that aren’t growing fast enough to justify their bloated valuation**, yet still have cash (or can cut costs) to avoid outright failure for now. Often they raised huge war chests – Gurley mentions an average of $200–300M raised per unicorn, implying many have multi-year runways【7†L188-L196】【7†L194-L202】. Paradoxically, the very fact that *too much* money was raised has extended their survival. Easy capital in the ZIRP era produced an environment where “companies that should have been eliminated by market forces still exist”【6†L63-L71】【6†L65-L73】. When funding is abundant, competition in a given sector proliferates – rather than one or two winners emerging, four or five well-funded players might coexist, none able to decisively beat the others. This **crowded market** problem is evident in ridesharing, food delivery, fintech, and other sectors where venture funds poured billions. Additionally, overfunding allowed companies to postpone the discipline of sustainable economics. Many “zombiecorns” are still burning cash and have *not* achieved profitability (some not even close). A study cited by Stanford professor Ilya Strebulaev found that of the unicorn cohort that arose in 2021’s boom, only a handful had managed an IPO by 2024, and several had already gone **bankrupt or sold for pennies on the dollar**【21†L92-L100】. Well-known examples include **Impossible Foods** (the plant-based meat unicorn) and **MasterClass** (celebrity online courses) – both were valued in the billions during the pandemic hype, only to see demand fall short. Impossible Foods had to slash staff and reportedly saw its valuation plunge by ~89% in secondary trades【21†L100-L108】【21†L102-L107】. MasterClass similarly laid off half its employees as its $2.8B peak valuation collapsed (it struggled to find new funding and was reportedly worth a fraction of that by 2023)【21†L100-L108】【21†L108-L115】. These are emblematic zombie unicorns: once high-flyers now essentially stuck, unable to raise more at sensible terms and not attractive enough for acquisition or IPO.

The **implications** of a thousand zombie unicorns are significant. For one, they represent a massive overhang of unrealized investments. PitchBook and CB Insights data suggest total *private-market* “unicorn equity” reached over **$5 trillion in implied value by 2024**, much of which is doubtful to ever be realized at face value【17†L195-L200】. This overhang weighs on venture portfolio returns for recent fund vintages – the record 2020–21 deployments may yield poor results if many unicorns end up exiting at heavy discounts. It also ties up talent and capital in limbo. Some argue it’s *healthier* for the ecosystem to have underperformers shake out (via down-rounds, acquisitions, or shutdowns) so that resources can recycle to new startups. Instead, zombies continuing to operate at subscale can **crowd the field**, making it harder for new disruptors to break in. Gurley described how over-financing a sector leads to *too many players*, all limping along and making industry consolidation “more difficult because the number of surviving companies increases from 1–2 to 3–5”【7†L273-L281】. We see this in markets like scooter rentals, food delivery, or scooter companies – none can gain a monopoly, yet none die quickly either, as they keep getting extended by venture lifelines. 

Another side effect is the **delay of IPOs** for the genuinely strong companies. If even mediocre unicorns manage to stay private (waiting for a more favorable market), the top-tier startups feel less pressure to rush public as well. Some high-quality unicorns like Stripe or Databricks postponed their IPOs and instead raised large private rounds in 2023–24, partly because the public markets were unfavorable but also because so much private capital was available that they *could* remain private. This further **concentrates growth in private hands**, denying public market investors access to high-growth firms until much later in their lifecycle (if ever). In 1999, a company like Amazon IPO’d at < $500M market cap; today many companies don’t go public until reaching $10B+ valuations (if at all), meaning the *public* misses out on the early growth phase returns【8†L33-L40】. Gurley has criticized this shift, noting that late-stage private rounds (often led by big institutions or sovereign funds) are effectively capturing returns that used to accrue after the IPO. For instance, whereas Amazon’s value grew exponentially as a public stock, nowadays a company might do most of its value appreciation in opaque private markets before any public listing【8†L31-L39】【8†L37-L40】. This trend raises questions about fairness and transparency – an issue even regulators like the SEC’s Hester Peirce have pondered (she has speculated about tokenizing private securities to create new pathways to liquidity and democratize access【8†L57-L65】【8†L61-L69】).

Ultimately, a **reckoning** for zombie unicorns seems inevitable. As one venture commentator put it, *“the unicorn boom is over, and startups are getting desperate”*【18†L15-L18】. Either these firms will find a way to grow into their valuations (few will), muddle along and eventually accept down-round financings/mergers, or run out of cash and die. We’ve started to see this playing out: by 2023–24, a number of high-profile unicorns that couldn’t justify their worth either quietly sold for a fraction of prior value (e.g. **Instacart** IPO’ed in 2023 at ~$10B, down ~75% from its last private valuation【21†L93-L100】) or shut down. The **“survival period”** has been extended by cheap capital, but not indefinitely【6†L63-L71】. Gurley’s advice is implicitly that founders/investors should *confront reality sooner*. If a company is fundamentally not living up to its $1B price tag, it’s better to reset expectations and pivot or restructure, rather than exist as a zombie that ties up resources. Some could potentially revive – with sharp strategic focus and cost cuts, a few may transform into viable businesses (the *Inc.* piece suggests going back to basics and *“mastering unit economics”* as a cure【21†L118-L126】【21†L133-L141】). But others simply don’t have a path to justify the hype. As Gurley notes, not every unicorn can be a *real* unicorn – many are destined to be valued like workhorses in the end.

## The IPO and M&A Market Stalemate

Hand-in-hand with the unicorn backlog is the **drought of exits** in recent years. Gurley emphasizes that from 2022 onward, the traditional liquidity routes – initial public offerings (IPOs) and mergers & acquisitions (M&A) – largely **shut down**, creating a bottleneck. The data bears this out starkly. In the U.S., **2021** was a banner year for venture-backed exits, with a record **$774 billion** in exit value generated【24†L39-L42】 (across IPOs, SPACs, and acquisitions) as tech stocks soared. Then the music stopped. In **2022**, U.S. venture exit value **collapsed by ~90%** to around $76B – the first year below $100B since 2016【25†L63-L70】. By **2023**, the situation was even more dire: only about **$64 billion** in VC-backed exits occurred in the entire year【25†L63-L70】. To put that in perspective, 2021’s exit haul was *over 12 times* larger. The number of VC-backed IPOs in 2022–2023 cratered to a tiny handful; an astonishing fact is that **for nearly two years no major venture-backed tech company went public in the U.S. after Dec 2021**【25†L81-L89】. Even as the Nasdaq rallied in 2023 (up ~30% that year【9†L318-L326】), the IPO window remained essentially *closed*. “That doesn’t make sense,” Gurley observes – historically a rising stock market is accompanied by active IPOs, but not this time【9†L323-L331】【9†L327-L335】.

What caused this **stalemate**? A confluence of factors, both structural and cyclical. First, **public market investors re-rated growth companies** once interest rates rose. The end of ZIRP in 2022 led to a sharp selloff in high-multiple tech stocks. By 2022–23, many recently IPO’d startups (2020–21 cohort) were trading well below their offering prices, souring the appetite for new listings. The IPO pipeline thus froze as companies (and their VC backers) did not want to debut at the lower valuations public markets would demand. Gurley notes that many hot startups in 2021 had been assigned *extremely high prices* by private investors, and those valuation expectations carried into 2022【9†L359-L367】. Even when market conditions improved in 2024, founders perceived the **“cost of going public”** as too high – not just regulatory/compliance costs, but the IPO discount and dilution. Venture insiders often complain that Wall Street’s IPO process significantly underprices offerings to benefit new buyers. According to data Gurley cites, the typical IPO in recent years involved a ~25% pricing discount plus ~7% in underwriting fees – meaning a company effectively *leaves 30%+ of its value on the table* when listing【8†L45-L53】. In a private mega-round, by contrast, an issuer might raise money at a premium valuation and with more control. Indeed, Gurley recounts a case where a CEO was advised by bankers to IPO at price X, and the founder retorted that **he could raise $1B at 20% higher valuation in the private market** immediately【8†L47-L55】. This illustrates why successful late-stage companies felt “no rush to go public”【9†L331-L339】 – *easy private capital* (from the mega-funds and crossover investors) allowed them to defer IPO indefinitely. 

The **M&A side** similarly hit a wall. Normally, if IPOs are tough, acquisitions might pick up slack (large tech firms acquiring startups). But regulatory headwinds and valuation mismatches curtailed that. Gurley points to antitrust enforcement: regulators in the U.S. (FTC Chair Lina Khan) and Europe signaled strong opposition to Big Tech buying potential rivals【9†L337-L345】. Large acquirers like Google, Amazon, Apple – the “seven giants” collectively sitting on hundreds of billions in cash – became *extremely cautious* about attempting major deals【9†L337-L345】【9†L339-L347】. The few big acquisitions attempted have faced long reviews or blocks. For example, in mid-2024 Google announced a $32 billion acquisition of cybersecurity firm **Wiz**, but immediately acknowledged it might take over a year to clear approvals【9†L343-L350】. Such uncertainty dampens M&A appetite; boards are reluctant to enter year-long limbo, and many deals die in the consideration phase. Compounding this, the **lofty valuations** of 2021 created a gap between what sellers want and what buyers will pay. Many startup boards still mentally reference the high watermark valuation (as do their VCs, who don’t want to accept an exit that implies a loss relative to last round). Meanwhile, potential acquirers see more reasonable public tech multiples and *refuse to pay unicorn prices*. This impasse means few big-ticket acquisitions of VC-backed firms have occurred since 2022. Gurley notes that even in 2024, there were “no record-breaking mergers” in the first half, despite cash-rich tech giants – because neither regulators nor boards would green-light them【9†L337-L345】.

The net result is a **“traffic jam” of startups** awaiting exits【25†L49-L57】. As of early 2025, analysts estimate hundreds of late-stage private companies in the U.S. alone are essentially IPO-ready (in terms of scale) but are holding off. Some have explored *alternative routes*: a mini-wave of direct listings and SPAC mergers in 2020-21 offered other paths, but those dried up when SPACs fell out of favor and direct listings remained rare. So, the ventures remain private – leading to the “private is the new public” paradigm Gurley discusses【9†L331-L339】. Companies like **SpaceX** and **ByteDance** have valuations in the stratosphere (SpaceX around $150B, ByteDance even higher) that, in another era, would likely make them public companies by now. Gurley mused about the prospect of a **trillion-dollar private company** – it sounds crazy, but SpaceX and perhaps OpenAI are on trajectories that could get there in a few years if they don’t IPO【9†L349-L357】. If that happens, it fundamentally challenges the notion that going public is a necessary or even desired milestone.

This *exit logjam* has far-reaching **implications**. First, it starves LPs of liquidity (discussed more in the next section). Venture funds can’t distribute stock or cash from exits that aren’t happening, which means LPs aren’t seeing returns on paper become actual cash returns. By late 2023, U.S. venture funds had gone through one of the worst periods of distribution in recent history – annual VC-backed exit value hit a **seven-year low of ~$120B globally in 2023【35†L199-L207】**, and North America’s share was only ~$66B【25†L63-L70】. In fact, each quarter of 2023 saw *less than $5B* in exit value for U.S. startups – extraordinarily low output【22†L73-L81】. This clearly challenged the “free-flowing capital” model of venture: with money not coming out, less new money goes in【22†L71-L78】. Indeed, U.S. VC fundraising dropped to about $67B in 2023 (the lowest since 2017) as LPs grew cautious【22†L79-L87】. Second, when companies stay private longer, **governance and transparency issues** can arise. Private markets lack the disclosure requirements of public ones; concerns like accurate valuation marks, as noted, become murky. The WeWork saga in 2019 was a cautionary tale of a hyped unicorn imploding only when it attempted an IPO and public investors scrutinized its finances. With today’s stalemate, many unicorns avoid that scrutiny, for better or worse.

However, by 2024–2025 there were **signs of a thaw**. Improved macro conditions (interest rates stabilizing, inflation easing) and strong stock market performance started to reopen the exit markets slightly【39†L33-L41】【29†L183-L192】. In late 2023, a few venture-backed IPOs like **Instacart** (CART) and **Klaviyo** (KVYO) did occur – albeit at much lower valuations than their 2021 private rounds, they at least broke the ice【25†L82-L90】. In early 2025, optimism grew that the backlog might finally clear: the first quarter of 2025 saw over **400 VC exits totaling $56B** (including several sizeable acquisitions), which on an annualized pace would far exceed 2023’s total【29†L182-L190】. Notable tech IPO filings were in the works – Reddit, for example, was reported to be preparing to list in 2025 alongside other decacorns【29†L203-L211】. And indeed M&A showed life, with corporate buyers starting to step up activity in sectors like fintech and SaaS where they could acquire growth at saner prices【29†L214-L223】. There is a broad expectation that **M&A will lead the way** in providing exits (since IPO markets are slower to normalize)【29†L214-L223】. Strategics with ample cash may begin cherry-picking the best distressed unicorns at *“more reasonable valuations”*, as one report noted【29†L214-L223】. Venture veterans caution, though, that we’re unlikely to return to the go-go exit multiples of 2021 anytime soon【29†L220-L227】【29†L222-L230】. The reset in pricing is real – startups and investors have to accept lower multiples and perhaps more modest outcomes.

One interesting consequence of the stalemate is the exploration of **alternative exit avenues**. There’s renewed talk of things like secondary market liquidity, tokenization, and direct acquisitions of minority stakes. For instance, Nasdaq private markets and other platforms facilitated more secondary sales of unicorn stock (employees or early investors selling shares to secondaries funds) while IPOs were shut. Regulators like SEC’s Peirce, as noted, even mused that blockchain tokens could someday allow startups to reach retail investors without a formal IPO【8†L57-L65】【8†L61-L68】. While still speculative, these ideas underscore a desire to *innovate* a way out of the logjam. Additionally, some companies chose **mergers** as a creative exit – e.g. merging two struggling unicorns to form a viable public candidate. We may see more private-to-private consolidation if the status quo persists. The key point is that the traditional pathways were blocked, forcing everyone to adapt.

In summary, the **IPO/M&A stalemate** since 2022 has been a pressure cooker for the venture ecosystem. It contributed to the “zombification” of startups (as discussed) and put LPs and VCs in a bind. But it appears to be slowly easing by mid-2025. Gurley’s analysis is that something *had* to give: you can’t have a perpetually rising stock market and hundreds of large startups with no exits – eventually either valuations adjust or new mechanisms emerge. Indeed, he points out the correlation that held for decades (NASDAQ up -> more IPOs) was bent, but likely not broken forever【9†L323-L331】【9†L329-L337】. The backlog of quality companies will ultimately demand liquidity, and when the window truly opens, we could see a **rush of IPO filings** and acquisitions – a wave of pent-up supply coming to market【29†L232-L241】【29†L234-L241】. That could be chaotic but also healthy, finally clearing the system. Investors (both VC and public) are watching closely for this inflection point. In the meantime, patience and **“playing the long game”** is the mantra for founders – focus on building sustainable businesses so you can survive until an exit opportunity appears.

## The AI Wave and Its Impact on Venture Capital

Just as the venture industry was bracing for a full correction in 2022–23, a new **technological gold rush** arrived: artificial intelligence. Gurley describes the timing of the **AI wave** (particularly the explosion of interest in generative AI) as almost *“opportune”* – it hit right when the startup world was tightening belts, preventing what might have been a deeper washout of weak companies【9†L426-L435】【9†L436-L443】. Indeed, the late-2022 debut of OpenAI’s ChatGPT and the ensuing breakthroughs in large language models (LLMs) set off *ferocious* investor enthusiasm. By 2023 and especially 2024, **AI became the hottest theme in venture capital**, reinvigorating funding activity that had slumped after the 2021 bubble. 

Some remarkable statistics: In 2024, global VC funding **rebounded ~30%** year-on-year after the 2022–23 dip【35†L167-L175】, and this was *largely driven by AI*. Nearly **46% of all venture dollars in 2024 went into AI-related startups** – a record concentration【35†L170-L178】【35†L172-L179】. Total investment in AI startups reached **$130+ billion in 2024**, up more than 50% from the prior year【34†L13-L18】【35†L167-L175】. Essentially, **AI sucked up almost half of venture capital globally**【39†L35-L43】【39†L36-L39】. The fervor spanned everything from core AI model developers to applied AI software and chip companies. In the U.S., this led to an uptick in new unicorn formation in 2024 (110 new unicorns globally, vs 100 in 2023), even as other sectors saw much less momentum【17†L175-L183】【17†L177-L185】. Notably, **65 of those new unicorns were U.S.-based and many were AI-focused**, whereas China’s creation of new unicorns actually slowed in that period【17†L175-L183】【17†L183-L191】. This signaled that *America seized a leadership position in the AI startup race* – a reversal from the 2015–2020 era when China often led in sheer number of new unicorns. 

The **enthusiasm is grounded in the idea that AI represents a once-in-a-generation platform shift**. Gurley and others compare it to the advent of the internet or mobile computing – a general-purpose technology that can transform every industry. He notes that “everyone believes” AI could be the **biggest technological platform change of our lifetime**【9†L367-L375】【9†L373-L381】. This belief, in turn, has a profound effect on investor psychology: if a startup is perceived as potentially the next *Google* or *Meta* of the AI era, there is almost *no price too high* for some investors【9†L371-L378】【9†L373-L381】. Gurley observed that in contrast to 30 years ago (when the power of network effects and compounding growth was underappreciated), today’s investors have seen small startups turn into trillion-dollar giants. So they are **willing to “price in” huge future expectations** for anything that even hints at such scale potential【9†L371-L378】. This mindset contributed to some eye-popping private valuations in AI: for example, **OpenAI** secured funding at a reported ~$30 billion valuation in 2023 and then more funding in 2024 (Microsoft’s investment totaling $10B) – extraordinary for a company with modest revenue. **Elon Musk’s xAI** launched in 2023 and by 2024 was said to be valued around **$12 billion** despite being pre-product【35†L181-L189】【35†L182-L189】. Other AI startups like Anthropic, Inflection, and Character.AI all raised at multi-billion valuations rapidly. Investors essentially decided these could be foundational companies of a new AI age, and **fear of missing out (FOMO)** on the “next big one” drove valuations to extremes – reminiscent of dot-com era fervor.

There are clear **parallels to previous tech hype cycles**, and Gurley voices a cautious note even amid the excitement. He acknowledges that if AI truly is transformative, *some* exuberance is warranted – “we should be excited” about it【9†L436-L443】. AI has already delivered real breakthroughs (the capabilities of GPT-4, etc.) that justify high expectations. Moreover, AI is not a zero-sum winner-takes-all space yet; opportunities span countless applications. This breadth attracted capital not just to a few giants but across the spectrum – from enterprise AI SaaS to AI biotech to autonomous vehicles (which increasingly are seen as an AI problem). In Q1 2025, one report noted **53% of global VC funding went to AI-related companies** – an astonishing concentration, showing AI essentially *dominated* venture conversations【34†L4-L12】【34†L5-L13】. 

However, industry veterans caution that **hype can run far ahead of reality**. For instance, many AI startups have negligible revenue and face **massive burn rates** (training large models and handling AI workloads is compute-intensive and expensive). As Franklin Templeton’s James Cross commented, the funding environment for “AI/LLM companies” was *historically rich* in 2023–24, but these firms *“will need to smash very significant business milestones”* to justify continued “infinity capital” access【35†L187-L195】. In other words, the bar is now very high – simply having a cool demo won’t be enough in a year or two once the initial wonder fades; customers and monetization must materialize. There are already signs of **bubble-like behavior**: some investors have poured money into any startup branding itself with “AI”, reminiscent of the blockchain craze in 2017 or the dot-com bubble in 1999 when adding “.com” boosted valuations. Gurley himself cautioned that the AI wave *interrupted the market correction* that was underway【11†L1-L9】 – effectively inflating a new mini-bubble before the previous excesses fully washed out. He warned all parties to be *vigilant about fundamentals and unit economics even amid the AI frenzy*【6†L73-L81】【6†L74-L78】. This is sage advice: historically, transformative technologies (from railroads to the internet) go through speculative manias where many participants go bust even though the technology does change the world. The *dot-com bust* of 2000 is a classic example – the internet did fulfill its promise, but not before wiping out a generation of overvalued startups around 1999–2001.

One key difference with AI is the **role of incumbents**. Unlike past platform shifts where startups clearly led (think social media or mobile apps), in AI the established tech giants (Google, Microsoft, Amazon, Meta) are deeply invested and arguably ahead in certain areas. Microsoft’s partnership with OpenAI gave it a large stake in the leading AI model provider; Google has its own advanced models (and acquired DeepMind years ago). This means some of the value in AI may accrue to big public companies rather than startups – or at least, the big firms may acquire or outcompete smaller players. That dynamic can cap how far VCs are willing to go, especially for core AI infrastructure companies. Nonetheless, big firms can’t build everything, so plenty of startups aiming at specific applications (AI in health, finance, creativity, etc.) still attract funding. 

**Internationally**, the AI race is a strategic focus for many countries, adding another layer to venture implications. The U.S. and China are clearly the front-runners. As noted, China’s creation of new unicorns slowed in 2023–24【17†L183-L191】【17†L185-L193】 – partly due to a broader tech funding slowdown and regulatory crackdowns in China – but China remains the second-largest locus of AI funding in absolute terms【17†L183-L191】. Chinese tech giants (Baidu, Alibaba, Tencent) and government initiatives are heavily investing in AI research, and a crop of Chinese AI startups (e.g. companies working on large language models for Chinese) have emerged, though often with state support. Gurley, interestingly, has been paying attention to China’s AI innovation; he noted that China’s intensely competitive environment in sectors like EVs and solar has bred strong companies, and a similar pattern could happen in AI【6†L81-L89】【6†L83-L87】. Europe, in contrast, has fewer AI unicorns and more regulatory scrutiny (the EU AI Act), but places like the UK and France have some notable AI labs and startups. The **geopolitical dimension** of AI (e.g. export controls on chips, U.S.-China rivalry) also can affect venture investing – U.S. chip startups like those designing AI semiconductors have benefited from huge interest as nations seek to secure their own AI hardware supply.

From a venture market perspective, the AI wave has had a **two-fold impact**: it *rekindled investor optimism* (spurring new fund launches, revitalizing fundraising in late 2024) and it *shifted capital allocation* strongly toward AI-related fields. Corporate VC also ramped up – many big companies started an AI venture fund or increased venture investing to not miss out (corporates participated in 35% of deal value in 2024, the highest in years【39†L41-L48】【39†L41-L45】, much of that in AI deals). This gusher of money has extended runway for some startups that otherwise might have struggled. Gurley mentions that by early 2023 most startups were in belt-tightening mode, cutting burn and aiming for breakeven given the tough funding climate【9†L426-L435】. But once AI fever took hold, growth and “vision” came back into vogue – companies started pitching themselves as AI-transformative and raised new funds, sometimes at rebounded valuations. In effect, **AI became an “excuse” for investors to open their checkbooks again**, resetting the cycle.

Looking forward, the key question is how much of the AI boom is durable. If AI truly is as fundamental as electricity or the microchip, then the value created could be enormous – justifying *some* of the sky-high valuations. For example, if an AI startup achieves a breakthrough in artificial general intelligence or dominates AI cloud services, it could be a trillion-dollar company eventually. But there will also be many disappointments and me-too companies that fail. Gurley’s advice is implicitly to **scrutinize the quality of revenue in AI companies** (e.g. are they selling real product or just custom projects? Are their models differentiated or easily commoditized by open-source?). He also discussed evaluating *“AI revenue quality”* and not blindly assuming every AI startup will have SaaS-like margins【6†L73-L81】【6†L74-L78】. As the hype tempers, strong business fundamentals will distinguish winners from the rest. 

In summary, the **AI wave injected new energy (and capital) into venture capital at a critical time**. It likely prevented a deeper downturn by redirecting investors’ attention to a promising new frontier. However, it also created a risk of **over-inflation** – a mini-bubble on top of the remnants of the previous one. Venture players must balance excitement for AI’s transformative potential with realism about execution and adoption timelines. As with past platform shifts, we will see both spectacular successes and expensive failures. Gurley’s take is that AI is *the* story of this era, and one must engage with it – but do so with eyes open. For VCs and founders, the implication is clear: leveraging AI’s power is now table stakes, but building a *defensible, profitable* business on AI is the real challenge ahead.

## Private Markets and LP Liquidity Issues

The freeze in exits and the explosion in private valuations have led to a serious, if underappreciated, consequence: **liquidity pressures on the investors behind the investors – the Limited Partners (LPs)**. These are the pensions, endowments, foundations, and wealthy family offices that supply capital to VC and private equity funds. Gurley identifies LP liquidity stress as a *new phenomenon* that emerged by 2023–2025, directly related to the closed IPO/M&A window【9†L380-L388】. In short, when VCs can’t exit their investments, **distributions to LPs dry up**, but the LPs are still on the hook for capital calls to new funds. This imbalance has become acute. By early 2025, many institutional LPs found themselves overextended in private assets and short on cash – even some of the savviest, like major university endowments.

A telling data point: in Q1 2025, U.S. universities raised **$12 billion in debt (bonds)**, the third-highest quarter ever for academia, to help fund their operations【9†L382-L390】【8†L25-L33】. Why are wealthy universities suddenly borrowing money? Because their endowments, heavily allocated to illiquid venture and PE investments, weren’t throwing off enough cash. Harvard University’s endowment, for example, announced plans to **sell $1 billion worth of its private equity holdings on the secondary market** to raise cash【33†L1-L4】. Even more striking, Yale – the pioneer of the heavy alternatives endowment model – signaled it would **sell up to $6 billion of its PE and VC assets** in secondary sales【33†L1-L4】. Yale’s move was momentous; as Gurley notes, *“no institution has had a greater impact on endowment strategy”* than Yale【9†L399-L407】, thanks to the late David Swensen’s model of allocating heavily to illiquid alternatives. If Yale is retrenching (for the first time engaging in a large secondary sale【32†L149-L158】【32†L151-L159】), it’s a clear sign that the liquidity crunch is real. As one secondary market publication put it, *the very school that championed illiquidity is now seeking liquidity* – perhaps indicating we’ve reached the limits of that model【32†L149-L158】【32†L151-L159】. In Yale’s case, one driver was unique political pressure (concerns about potential federal funding cuts and tariffs affecting endowments【32†L151-L159】【32†L153-L159】), but the broader driver was the need for cash to meet spending obligations when the portfolio isn’t generating cash-outs【32†L187-L193】【32†L189-L193】.

This scenario is often referred to as the **“denominator effect.”** When public markets fell in 2022, many LPs suddenly found that private assets (which didn’t get marked down as quickly) became an outsized percentage of their portfolio – overshooting their target allocation. Simultaneously, because exits halted, **LPs stopped receiving distributions** from VC/PE funds. In a normal year, mature venture funds return capital steadily via IPOs or acquisitions; LPs then recycle some of that into new fund commitments. But 2022–2023 saw an unprecedented collapse in VC distributions. For instance, in 2023 U.S. venture funds returned only ~$60B to LPs【22†L71-L78】, versus hundreds of billions in 2021. That means many LPs’ cash inflows from VC went to near-zero, right when they were expected to *continue* funding capital calls for newer vintage funds raised during the boom. The outcome: **liquidity strain**. Pensions and endowments still have to pay annual outlays (university budgets, pension benefits, etc.), and if their private investments aren’t returning cash, they must get that cash elsewhere – by selling assets or borrowing.

We saw this manifest in multiple ways: besides Harvard and Yale tapping secondaries or debt, numerous pension funds have been active sellers on the secondary market. For example, in late 2022 and 2023, large institutions like **CalPERS** and **Texas Teachers** were rumored to sell chunks of their private equity portfolios at discounts, aiming to rebalance and raise cash. The secondary market for LP interests boomed – by 2023 it was common to see portfolios trading at 10-20% discounts to NAV, yet still finding buyers as some investors (like secondary funds, or cash-rich groups like sovereign wealth funds) sought to snap up stakes cheaply. Gurley cites that even **prestigious endowments resorted to secondaries or debt** – a remarkable development【6†L65-L73】【6†L67-L71】. When LPs use **debt to fund capital calls or annual spending**, it’s a sign of stress: essentially, they’re leveraging up because the cash that was supposed to come from investments is delayed. This is likely a short-term tactic; they don’t want to *fire-sale* their holdings at huge discounts, so borrowing can bridge the gap, but it’s not sustainable if the drought persists.

The **magnitude** of the private asset boom sets the context. Venture capital became a much larger slice of institutional portfolios over the last decade – Gurley noted some LPs doubled their VC allocation from ~5% to 10-15% of assets【7†L194-L202】【7†L195-L200】. Industry data shows total venture AUM reached ~$3.5T globally by 2022【14†L129-L137】. This means LPs collectively have trillions “locked” in illiquid venture investments that they can’t easily cash out. NVCA reports estimated about **$3 trillion in book value** for LPs’ venture portfolios as of 2025【7†L188-L196】. In flush times, that wasn’t a concern – they were happy to let it ride. But as everyone piled into the Yale model (heavy illiquids), they did so under the assumption that illiquids *outperform* and that eventually cash comes back. Now, if everyone is overweight illiquids at the same time and exits stall, it creates a system-wide liquidity crunch. Gurley paraphrases Howard Marks: the great returns from being non-consensus only apply until everyone copies the strategy【9†L407-L415】. Here, *everyone* copied Yale, so being overloaded on privates is no longer contrarian – it’s the norm, meaning those excess returns might diminish while liquidity risk increases. 

One direct consequence is that **LPs have become more discerning and pull back from new commitments**. The 2023 drop in VC fundraising (less than $67B raised by U.S. VC funds, a steep fall from 2021【22†L79-L87】) reflects LPs saying “we need to slow down”. Some LPs outright paused investing in new funds or shrank ticket sizes. This hits emerging VC managers and smaller funds hardest (the big brand-name funds still raised, but even they sometimes took longer or accepted smaller fund sizes than planned). It also contributes to the “haves vs have-nots” dynamic mentioned earlier – mega-funds can still secure commitments (often from a concentrated group of large LPs), whereas newer or niche funds struggle. 

LP liquidity issues also drive more creative solutions: **secondary funds** (funds that specialize in buying LP stakes or direct secondary share purchases) saw a surge in deal flow. Firms like Blackstone, Ardian, and Goldman raised large secondary vehicles anticipating a wave of selling. By 2024, the secondaries market was one of the few active areas in private equity, as LPs sought liquidity and some VC-backed companies allowed secondary share sales to give early investors or employees an exit. The catch is pricing – many were sold at discounts of 20-30%, effectively acknowledging the overvaluation of 2021-era marks. Gurley mentions that the information LPs receive on valuations varies widely – some VC GPs mark down proactively, others don’t【7†L213-L221】. This inconsistency makes LPs wary; some may choose to sell stakes in funds they suspect are carrying assets too optimistically.

Another manifestation is that some institutional LPs have used **leveraged tactics historically and are feeling the pain**. For instance, certain endowments or private investors use subscription credit lines or even borrow against their endowment to meet commitments (taking advantage of low rates). As rates rose and liquidity fell, those strategies look riskier. The University of California made news in early 2023 by negotiating a special deal to invest in Sequoia’s fund with a preferred return – a sign that LPs are using their leverage to get better terms given the tougher climate. 

What are the **implications** if LP liquidity issues persist? One is that **venture fund sizes could moderate**. If LPs continue to tighten the tap, VCs will raise smaller funds or fewer funds, which eventually constrains capital in the startup ecosystem (though with a lag, since a lot of dry powder is still in the system from prior raises). Indeed, by late 2024 many VC firms slowed their fundraising cadence, some delaying new funds. Another implication is **opportunities for investors with liquidity** – e.g. sovereign wealth funds from oil-rich nations (Saudi Arabia’s PIF, Abu Dhabi’s Mubadala, etc.) or large asset managers might step in as LPs or secondary buyers. We have seen Middle Eastern funds actively investing directly in tech (for example, UAE and Saudi funds have backed many late-stage rounds, including AI companies). These players often have counter-cyclical capital – in a sense, they *became* the liquidity providers of last resort, much like how Japan’s SoftBank did via Vision Fund at the last cycle’s peak (though SoftBank itself had to retrench after heavy losses). Gurley even quips that **“Masa is back”** – Masayoshi Son of SoftBank returned to making big investments by 2024 after lying low during losses【7†L173-L180】. This suggests that even as traditional LPs pull back, some aggressive capital (often globally sourced) is still willing to jump in, which complicates the correction process. 

Finally, LP constraints may **force some structural innovations or shifts**. For instance, more VC funds might offer **continuation vehicles** or extended fund lives to avoid fire-sales of good assets. We already saw a trend of “VC secondaries” where firms roll over their best companies into a new vehicle (with new LP money) rather than selling or IPO – effectively delaying exit but giving original LPs a chance to cash out if they want. Also, **fee pressure** could emerge if LPs push back on paying high fees to funds that aren’t liquidating. LPs might also favor VCs who *distribute* regularly (e.g. those focusing on earlier exits or acquisitions) versus those that just mark-up paper value. In essence, a return to focus on *cash returns (TVPI to DPI conversion)* over just paper gains is likely.

In conclusion, the venture world’s liquidity crunch at the LP level is a delayed consequence of the boom’s excess and bust’s freeze. As Gurley underscores, it’s somewhat unprecedented to see elite endowments **“issuing bonds to cover 5% payouts”** or selling chunks of their crown-jewel VC funds【6†L65-L73】【6†L67-L71】. It suggests that the private market ecosystem is *not* infinitely self-sustaining; it ultimately relies on public market exits to complete the cycle of money. The current adjustments – secondaries, reduced allocations, etc. – are the system’s way of equilibrating. Over the next few years, if exits rebound, liquidity will improve and LPs may resume heavy commitments (albeit with more caution). If the drought were prolonged, more drastic changes could ensue (fund failures, LP defaults on capital calls, etc., which thankfully haven’t happened broadly yet). Gurley’s framing of it as a **system-wide coordination problem** is apt【6†L39-L47】【6†L45-L53】 – everyone acted individually rational during the boom (GPs raised big funds, LPs over-allocated to VC chasing high returns, founders took easy money), but collectively it created an imbalanced system. Now the unwinding requires coordination too, or at least time for things to settle. The hope is that with some high-profile liquidity events in 2025 and beyond, the pressure will ease. But LPs will likely remember this lesson and possibly manage their illiquidity exposure more carefully going forward.

---

Each of these major themes from Bill Gurley’s 2025 market analysis paints a picture of an industry in flux. Venture capital is navigating **unprecedented scale (mega-funds and trillion-dollar private valuations)**, grappling with the **fallout of a burst bubble (zombie unicorns and stalled exits)**, and simultaneously chasing the **next big revolution (AI)** – all under the shadow of macroeconomic shifts and liquidity constraints. For investors, founders, and analysts, the key takeaways are to remain *clear-eyed* about incentive structures and historical patterns. The boom times of 2020–21 created distortions that will take years to fully unwind, but also seeded innovation (some of which – like AI – could be truly transformative). The coming years will likely see a more **Darwinian sorting** of startups: those with solid fundamentals and prudent capital use should survive and eventually thrive, while the weakest will either die off or consolidate. Valuations will, in many cases, reset to more rational levels – a healthy process, even if painful for some.

From an LP and macro perspective, the venture ecosystem will also recalibrate. Capital is still abundant (by 2025 there remained significant “dry powder” in VC – an estimated $300B+ unused in the U.S.【39†L133-L141】【39†L139-L147】), but it’s deployed more cautiously now. The pendulum has swung from **“growth at all costs” to “show me the profits”**, as evidenced by rising revenue and profitability requirements for IPO candidates and even Series A companies【39†L45-L53】【39†L46-L54】. In 2021 a startup could raise millions on a concept slide; in 2025 investors expect traction and a path to break-even. This discipline, enforced by market conditions, should lead to more resilient companies in the long run. 

As Gurley’s analysis underscores, none of these trends exist in isolation – they interact as part of a complex system. Mega-funds contributed to higher valuations, which contributed to the unicorn glut, which in combination with macro factors led to the exit stalemate, which is stressing LPs, which in turn feeds back into how funds operate. And cutting across all of it is the wildcard of technological change – AI injecting both opportunities and volatility. For a knowledgeable audience of VCs, founders, and LPs, the value in this discussion is connecting the dots: understanding how **incentives and capital flows** at each level create the outcomes we see, and drawing lessons from past cycles (dot-com bust, 2008 recession, etc.) to inform current strategy. Gurley’s parting advice in the podcast was reportedly aimed at founders navigating this landscape: be aware of these realities, **play the game that exists today, not yesterday’s game**【6†L45-L53】, and position yourself for the way it may evolve tomorrow. In practice, that means balancing ambition with realism – raising sufficient capital but not overcapitalizing, pursuing disruptive innovation (like AI) but keeping an eye on sustainable unit economics, and being ready to seize exit opportunities when they arise (because windows can close quickly). 

The venture market of 2025 is undoubtedly challenging, but it is also maturing. Just as prior shake-outs in technology ultimately paved the way for the next wave of innovation, the current reset – if managed well by investors and entrepreneurs – can lay the groundwork for a healthier, more **fundamentals-driven** era in the late 2020s. And if history is any guide, those who adapt to these new realities will be poised to build and invest in the most enduring companies of the future.

metadata.title: The Evolution of Venture Capital Fund Sizes - Chronograph
metadata.text: The ascent in valuations and funding rounds has set in motion a positive feedback loop for larger funds — higher price tags mean GPs now need to write bigger checks. Moreover, with companies securing increased capital at elevated valuations across all stages, the dilution post-seed investment has significantly amplified. Maintaining double-digit ownership in a fund’s winners today simply requires a significantly larger seed fund than was needed in recent years. For example, a GP who raised $50 million in 2015 would likely need a $75 million fund to sustain the same level of ownership at current entry valuations.

metadata.title: The Exit Is Closed: Why Venture Capital Can’t Cash Out – The Clockwork Orbit
metadata.text: The U.S. venture capital ecosystem has been hit by an unprecedented drought in exits during the last 4 years. In 2023, U.S. venture-backed exits returned $64B to investors, a tiny fraction of the record sums seen in 2021 (4). During Q3 and Q4 2022, quarterly exit value plunged to $8B marking them the worst quarters for VC liquidity since 2013 (5). This collapse comes from a 90% decline in exit value in 2022 versus the 2021 boom amid inflation concerns and rising interest rates. In fact, 2022’s annual exit tally of $76B was the first time since 2016 that U.S. venture exit value fell below $100B (6).

metadata.title: The Exit Is Closed: Why Venture Capital Can’t Cash Out – The Clockwork Orbit
metadata.text: In the first quarter of 2025, the U.S. venture capital industry finds itself stuck in an unusual predicament: startups are plentiful, yet exits have nearly come to a halt. Companies that once eagerly planned to go public or merge with larger firms now hesitate, wary of the uncertain economic landscape. This hesitation has created a traffic jam of startups waiting for the right moment to exit, leaving investors and founders alike wondering when conditions will finally improve. As the market cautiously eyes signs of recovery, predictions emerge that the logjam might soon ease, but no one is yet certain when or how quickly this will come.

metadata.text: Bill: The first thing I want to mention, which everyone is talking about, is the continued rise of super venture capital funds. When I first entered the industry, everything was very customized, and most well-known funds focused on early-stage investments. metadata.title: The Evolution of Venture Capital Fund Sizes - Chronograph
metadata.text: The numbers speak volumes: venture’s assets under management (AUM) surged from $300 billion in 2008 to a staggering $3.5 trillion in 2022. Over the same period, the number of venture capital firms more than tripled from 1,000 to 4,000, and venture fundraising skyrocketed from $48 billion in 2008 to a peak of $363 billion in 2021. Today, venture capital has ascended to a mainstream investment strategy, representing almost 25% of total private capital AUM.

metadata.title: The Evolution of Venture Capital Fund Sizes - Chronograph
metadata.text: With more capital flowing into the hands of managers, larger fund sizes have naturally followed. In 2013, the average fund size was $84 million; by Q4 2023, that figure climbed to a whopping $153.8 million. The pronounced uptick can largely be attributed to the emergence of mega-funds in recent years, driven by headline-making fundraises from industry giants like Andreessen Horowitz, Tiger Global, and, notably, Softbank’s $100 billion Vision Fund.

metadata.title: The Evolution of Venture Capital Fund Sizes - Chronograph
metadata.text: In 2013, the average pre-money valuation for a seed round stood at $5.3 million. By 2023, this figure tripled to $18.4 million. This surge in seed-stage valuations has reverberated across subsequent stages, with the average pre-money valuation for early-stage rounds (Series A & B) jumping from $22.9 million in 2013 to $78.1 million in 2023. Naturally, this escalation has also driven up financing round sizes across all stages, with later stages experiencing particularly significant increases.

These companies are burning through their cash and are struggling to find a way out—possibly by merging with a healthier company.

metadata.title: The Exit Is Closed: Why Venture Capital Can’t Cash Out – The Clockwork Orbit
metadata.text: The U.S. venture capital ecosystem has been hit by an unprecedented drought in exits during the last 4 years. In 2023, U.S. venture-backed exits returned $64B to investors, a tiny fraction of the record sums seen in 2021 (4). During Q3 and Q4 2022, quarterly exit value plunged to $8B marking them the worst quarters for VC liquidity since 2013 (5). This collapse comes from a 90% decline in exit value in 2022 versus the 2021 boom amid inflation concerns and rising interest rates. In fact, 2022’s annual exit tally of $76B was the first time since 2016 that U.S. venture exit value fell below $100B (6).
